metadata.title: Trolls without borders: a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns

abstract: Despite the existence of multiple propaganda campaigns from around the globe, most scholarly attention has been primarily given to those operated by Russia. This focus on a single country has limited the scope of propaganda research. We offer a systematic comparison of six campaigns, examining the issues they engaged with, and their deployment strategies. Using a computational analysis of more than 9.5 million tweets released by the Twitter Moderation Research Consortium, we show differences across campaigns. Some operations were massive in scale while others were modest. Some focused on US domestic affairs, while others emphasized global issues. While some campaigns showed high levels of sophistication in terms of persona crafting and account deployment, others did not participate in pre-propaganda. We conclude by discussing the practical and theoretical implications of these findings.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: Myriad studies by academics and US intelligence agencies have established that the Russian state-operated Internet Research Agency (IRA) operated and facilitated a rich network of fabricated accounts in recent years (Jamieson, 2018). Specifically, the IRA aimed to meddle with US politics, influence US public opinion, and affect the outcome of the 2016 elections. This complex and intensive online activity was often explained through the lens propaganda theory (Golovchenko et al., 2020;Lukito, 2020;Woolley, 2016). The understanding of the content and potential effects of such foreign political interference was primarily based on the Russian case (Broniatowski et al., 2018;DiResta et al., 2019;Farkas & Bastos, 2018;Freelon et al., 2020;Golovchenko et al., 2020;Jamieson, 2018;Linvill et al., 2019;Lukito, 2020;Lukito et al., 2020;Rains et al., 2021;Walter et al., 2020).

While Russia was far from being the only country mounting persuasive online campaigns targeting American public opinion, other campaigns, especially ones originating in the Global South (Bradshaw & Howard, 2017;Woolley, 2016), received little scientific scrutiny and remain understudied. We argue that while the slew of Russian-focused studies had served to turn a spotlight on the growing threats associated with digital propaganda, it had also limited the scope of analysis, narrowing our theoretical understanding of contemporary propaganda and its implications (Jowett & O'Donnell, 2011). In response, we offer a comparative analysis of six online foreign propaganda campaigns, all of which were identified as malicious and released by Twitter. Focusing on six countries, rather than one, we attempt to better understand the characteristics of contemporary propaganda.

In terms of theory development, we believe that the overreliance on Russia as a case study may have led to a narrow conceptualization of digital propaganda and the political and cultural arguments that such efforts may convey to American citizens. According to the Twitter's Election Integrity database, 1 many international actors have conducted lengthy, sophisticated online campaigns aimed at public opinion manipulation (Golovchenko et al., 2020;Linvill & Warren, 2020). We expect differences across campaigns for multiple reasons. First, different countries may craft Western-targeted propaganda for different reasons, based on varying geopolitical goals. For example, three of the studied countries here, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Iran, and Saudi Arabia, had been involved in decades of complex, often tensed, relationships with the West and with the United States in particular. Propaganda by and against these countries played a key role in these international affairs, which was primarily studied with a focus on US propaganda directed at other countries (Jones, 2019). At the time of writing, some of the countries serve as American adversaries (e.g., Iran), while others, like Saudi Arabia, maintain economic, political, and military relationships and collaborations with the West. We expect campaigns originating in countries maintaining tense or adversarial relationships with the United States to dedicate substantially more resources to US-targeted propaganda, than countries who cooperate with the United States in diplomatic avenues. Even among adversaries, we expect differences in the political goals of US-targeted propaganda. For example, while the Russians were working to promote Republican Presidential hopeful Donald Trump's campaign in 2016 (Walter et al., 2020), Venezuela often conflicted with the Trump administration (Briceño-Ruiz, 2019). Therefore, we expect different campaigns to focus on different topics, themes, arguments, and sentiment.

Second, some of the countries in our corpus, such as Russia and Iran, deployed propaganda campaigns in more than one country. For example, China was heavily involved with campaigns in South East Asia, in countries such as Hong Kong, or the Philippines. Saudi Arabia was mostly involved in campaigns targeting the Arab world, in countries such as Jordan or Egypt (Bradshaw et al., 2020). Other countries, such as the UAE and Venezuela, operated more modest campaigns. As a result, some countries, such as Russia (The IRA) and Iran (The Supreme Council of Cyberspace) maintained robust propaganda offices and units (Bradshaw & Howard, 2023), while others worked with more limited resources. Moreover, as demonstrated by Bradshaw et al. (2020), the countries studies here differed in the types and magnitude of digital propaganda coming from nongovernmental sources, such as civil society organizations, and private contractors.

Following others (e.g., Bradshaw & Howard, 2023), we argue that a comparative analysis is required in order to better understand modern online propaganda. At the time of writing, existing comparative literature was limited to domestic propaganda (Bradshaw & Howard, 2023;Woolley, 2016) with less comparative attention given to foreign campaigns. We argue that our study contributes not only to the development of a theoretical understanding of foreign propaganda, but also provides important practical insight for those countering foreign interference in American politics. While some prior scholarly work had examined propaganda efforts coming from Iran and China, very little is known about communications coming from Venezuela, UAE, and Saudi Arabia. For the latter, most research had focused on foreign propaganda campaigns directed at, and not created by, these countries (e.g., Kießling et al., 2019). In some cases, such as UAE, ours is the first to go beyond interviews with government officials (Antwi-Boateng, 2021;Bradshaw & Howard, 2023) to examine actual English-written propaganda content. Exploring the broader digital effort against the United States could help prepare intelligence agencies for coping with current and future propaganda attacks.

For theoretical consistency, in this article we follow other researchers, as well as the Twitter Content Moderation team, and refer to the operations studied here as campaigns. The word campaign emphasizes the orchestrated nature of operations, consisting of multiple state-sponsored interconnected accounts, working together to promote specific messaging. We differentiate between two distinct concepts: the target country, which refers to the citizens of a country which the campaign hopes to persuade, and the focus of the propaganda content, referring to the manifest content in actual campaign messages and its possible inferred goals. We thus pay attention to both the topics with which the campaigns engaged (the what), and the utilization of media (the how), including an exploration of the methods and steps taken to prepare the media environment to be more receptive to campaign messages, also known as pre-propaganda (Ellul & Kellen, 1973;Golovchenko et al., 2020;Jowett & O'Donnell, 2011).

(2020), the countries studies here differed in the types and magnitude of digital propaganda coming from nongovernmental sources, such as civil society organizations, and private contractors.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
sections.header_raw: Propaganda and foreign interference in politics
sections.header_norm: propaganda and foreign interference in politics
paragraphs: Since the rapid introduction and development of mass media at the beginning of the 20th century, scholars have been occupied with understanding the content and effects of propaganda and psychological warfare (Bernays, 1928). According to Lasswell (1938), propaganda is "the control of opinion by significant symbols" like "stories, rumors, reports, pictures, and other forms of social communication" (p. 9). As opposed to other types of communication, propaganda is multilayered, complex, intentional, planned, coercive, and systematic (Becker, 1949;Jowett & O'Donnell, 2011). Propaganda is often employed at times of war or conflict to influence the opinions and behaviors of both local and foreign audiences. Becker (1949) suggested classifying propaganda based on whether the source of persuasion is identifiable, i.e., whether activities are overt or covert (notice the classification is not based on accuracy or veracity of messages). More recently, Farkas and Neumayer (2018) offered the three-level taxonomy of identifiable (white), obfuscated (gray), and impersonated (black) propaganda. Another useful classification was suggested by Ellul and Kellen (1973) between vertical and horizontal propaganda. The former is administered and controlled by elites and formal institutions (e.g., political or religious) while the latter is made from the ground up in a nonhierarchical fashion. The contemporary online campaigns studied here employ vertical (as identified by the Department of Justice, 2018) impersonated (black) propaganda, with campaign agents harnessing the affordances of social media (Freelon et al., 2020;Linvill et al., 2019;Woolley, 2016) to masquerade as local users (i.e., impersonating the identity of the countries they target; Freelon et al., 2020;Linvill & Warren, 2020;Lukito et al., 2020). We thus focus our analysis on tweets written in English.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
sections.header_raw: The purpose of contemporary online propaganda
sections.header_norm: the purpose of contemporary online propaganda
paragraphs: The purpose of propaganda is to influence the opinions, beliefs, attitudes, and behaviors of a target population. The goal could be as concrete and specific as influencing voting or protesting behaviors (Jowett & O'Donnell, 2011), or abstract as sowing discord among the populace (Broniatowski et al., 2018;Ellul & Kellen, 1973). Oftentimes, the propaganda aims to encourage people to adopt the ideology of the propagandist (Jowett & O'Donnell, 2011), preferably without them becoming aware of the persuasion attempt (Laswell, 1938). As propaganda activities are often hard to directly observe, their nature is often studied and their goals inferred by an analysis of the content of their messages.

The complexity of contemporary online propaganda was demonstrated in analyses of Russian disinformation campaigns targeting Western social media platforms, dating back to least 2014 (Linvill & Warren, 2020). The sophisticated online network of social media accounts was operated and coordinated by the IRA, a Russian state-owned organization based in St. Petersburg (Jamieson, 2018). The IRA operated a comprehensive multilayered campaign, originating from a factory-like institute where work was done in constant shifts, and spread over multiple platforms, from Twitter (Linvill & Warren, 2020) and Facebook (Benkler et al., 2018) to YouTube (Golovchenko et al., 2020) and Reddit (Lukito, 2020).

While scholars agree on the existence and prevalence of IRA activity, they often disagree on the real-world impact of propaganda efforts. Due to the lack of empirical control in real-world settings, it remains unclear whether the final outcome of the 2016 US election, the triumph of Donald Trump, could be attributed to Russian interference. While some scholars argue that based on the circumstances, a Russian influence was theoretically plausible (Jamieson, 2018;Linvill et al., 2019), others argued that it is unlikely that the elections were determined by the Russians (Bail et al., 2020;Benkler et al., 2018). Others claimed that the IRA influenced American discourse that could be seen beyond the scope of electoral success of specific candidates, for example in mainstream media outlets, where Russian messages were discussed as if they were the opinions and ideas of American citizens (Vox Populi, see Lukito et al., 2020).

Similarly, scholars disagree on the motivations behind IRA activities. There is a consensus around the argument that IRA-distributed information favored Presidential Republican candidate Donald Trump and was aimed at harming his Democrat opponent, Hillary Clinton (Benkler et al., 2018;Jamieson, 2018). However, analyses found evidence for discussions of nonpolitical issues as well, ranging from popular culture to scientific topics like genetic engineering, fracking, and vaccines (Broniatowski et al., 2018;Walter et al., 2020). It remains unclear whether the underlying goal of such discussions was to sow discord among Americans, to secure a Trump victory, or to influence public opinion around specific topics, such as science (Benkler et al., 2018;Golovchenko et al., 2020;Jamieson, 2018;Walter et al., 2020).

What scholars do agree about, though, is that the IRA activity, at least the messages created in English, was aimed primarily at Americans (Freelon et al., 2020;Linvill & Warren, 2020;Rains et al., 2021), and focused primarily on topics relevant to the United States (Broniatowski et al., 2018;Freelon et al., 2020;Jamieson, 2018). In that regard, IRA activity differed from historical cases where propaganda offices were mostly concerned with the opinions and behaviors of the country's own citizens [a common practice in authoritarian regimes, see Ellul & Kellen (1973) and in the Global South today, see Bradshaw & Howard, 2017;Woolley, 2016] and is more in line with Cold War examples, such as the American employment of Radio Free Europe and Radio Liberty (Cone, 1999;Cummings, 2008), which were aimed at swaying public opinion in communist satellite states behind the Iron Curtain, against their own political systems. Propaganda aimed at foreign countries was often used during war times to weaken the resolve of the target country to fight the origin country (Ellul & Kellen, 1973), or to forge postwar Anglo-American cooperation (Brewer, 2019). Lastly, propaganda could be aimed at citizens of third-party countries that are not directly involved in the conflict. A notable example would be the British campaign at the beginning of the second World War aimed to breaking isolationism and riling up US support and involvement in the war with Germany (Cull, 1996), or Russian attempts to gain American support for the annexation of Crimea (Walter et al., 2020).

To sum, our understanding of contemporary online propaganda campaigns draws heavily on the Russian case study, and thus the targets studied were primarily US public opinion on domestic affairs. At the same time, very little is known about the topics and targets of online foreign campaigns from other countries, identified by the Twitter Integrity team, 2 including the countries studied here; Saudi Arabia, Iran, Venezuela, UAE, and China. Our efforts in the current study aim to fill this gap by examining whether different campaigns focused on different types of content in their online propaganda activity. We asked: RQ1: What were the issues that foreign countries' campaigns engaged with in their online English language propaganda on Twitter?

The sophisticated online network of social media accounts was operated and coordinated by the IRA, a Russian state-owned organization based in St. Petersburg (Jamieson, 2018). The IRA operated a comprehensive multilayered campaign, originating from a factory-like institute where work was done in constant shifts, and spread over multiple platforms, from Twitter (Linvill & Warren, 2020) and Facebook (Benkler et al., 2018) to YouTube (Golovchenko et al., 2020) and Reddit (Lukito, 2020).

While scholars agree on the existence and prevalence of IRA activity, they often disagree on the real-world impact of propaganda efforts. Due to the lack of empirical control in real-world settings, it remains unclear whether the final outcome of the 2016 US election, the triumph of Donald Trump, could be attributed to Russian interference. While some scholars argue that based on the circumstances, a Russian influence was theoretically plausible (Jamieson, 2018;Linvill et al., 2019), others argued that it is unlikely that the elections were determined by the Russians (Bail et al., 2020;Benkler et al., 2018). Others claimed that the IRA influenced American discourse that could be seen beyond the scope of electoral success of specific candidates, for example in mainstream media outlets, where Russian messages were discussed as if they were the opinions and ideas of American citizens (Vox Populi, see Lukito et al., 2020).

Similarly, scholars disagree on the motivations behind IRA activities. There is a consensus around the argument that IRA-distributed information favored Presidential Republican candidate Donald Trump and was aimed at harming his Democrat opponent, Hillary Clinton (Benkler et al., 2018;Jamieson, 2018). However, analyses found evidence for discussions of nonpolitical issues as well, ranging from popular culture to scientific topics like genetic engineering, fracking, and vaccines (Broniatowski et al., 2018;Walter et al., 2020). It remains unclear whether the underlying goal of such discussions was to sow discord among Americans, to secure a Trump victory, or to influence public opinion around specific topics, such as science (Benkler et al., 2018;Golovchenko et al., 2020;Jamieson, 2018;Walter et al., 2020).

What scholars do agree about, though, is that the IRA activity, at least the messages created in English, was aimed primarily at Americans (Freelon et al., 2020;Linvill & Warren, 2020;Rains et al., 2021), and focused primarily on topics relevant to the United States (Broniatowski et al., 2018;Freelon et al., 2020;Jamieson, 2018). In that regard, IRA activity differed from historical cases where propaganda offices were mostly concerned with the opinions and behaviors of the country's own citizens [a common practice in authoritarian regimes, see Ellul & Kellen (1973) and in the Global South today, see Bradshaw & Howard, 2017;Woolley, 2016] and is more in line with Cold War examples, such as the American employment of Radio Free Europe and Radio Liberty (Cone, 1999;Cummings, 2008), which were aimed at swaying public opinion in communist satellite states behind the Iron Curtain, against their own political systems. Propaganda aimed at foreign countries was often used during war times to weaken the resolve of the target country to fight the origin country (Ellul & Kellen, 1973), or to forge postwar Anglo-American cooperation (Brewer, 2019). Lastly, propaganda could be aimed at citizens of third-party countries that are not directly involved in the conflict. A notable example would be the British campaign at the beginning of the second World War aimed to breaking isolationism and riling up US support and involvement in the war with Germany (Cull, 1996), or Russian attempts to gain American support for the annexation of Crimea (Walter et al., 2020).

To sum, our understanding of contemporary online propaganda campaigns draws heavily on the Russian case study, and thus the targets studied were primarily US public opinion on domestic affairs. At the same time, very little is known about the topics and targets of online foreign campaigns from other countries, identified by the Twitter Integrity team, 2 including the countries studied here; Saudi Arabia, Iran, Venezuela, UAE, and China. Our efforts in the current study aim to fill this gap by examining whether different campaigns focused on different types of content in their online propaganda activity. We asked: RQ1: What were the issues that foreign countries' campaigns engaged with in their online English language propaganda on Twitter?

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
sections.header_norm: media utilization pre propaganda and deployment
paragraphs: In addition to the advantages of rapid, broad, and easy to create and access information, brought about by the Internet, social media also made it easy for propagandists to obscure their identity and intentions behind pseudo-local accounts (Jamieson, 2018). This allowed them to overcome one of the main challenges for propaganda communicators in the 20th century-the creation and production of reliable and credible information sources (Becker, 1949)-ones that foreign audiences will be willing to follow and listen to. To conceal their political intentions, IRA accounts published messages under fabricated American names and personas (Linvill et al., 2019), often using specific American dialects (Walter et al., 2020) or terms (e.g., usernames containing the Republican party's Great Old Party, GOP, acronym, see Jamieson, 2018). In addition, Russian accounts often bolstered the credibility of their fabricated users by first discussing what at the time was considered "soft," nonpolitical issues, such as vaccines (Walter et al., 2020), before shifting to politics. The technique was interpreted by Golovchenko et al. (2020) as a pre-propaganda, a deployment stage aimed at preparing the grounds for future political operations (Ellul & Kellen, 1973).

At the early stages of their campaign, IRA online communications included topics such as Star Wars films, vaccines (Walter et al., 2020), or hashtag games like responding to @Midnight prompts (Linvill et al., 2019). As demonstrated by Walter et al. (2020), the use of nonpolitical topics was strategic and not arbitrary, as accounts guised under different thematic personas (Walter et al., 2020) tended to focus on different topics relevant to that persona (Farkas & Bastos, 2018;Freelon et al., 2020;Golovchenko et al., 2020;Linvill & Warren, 2020;Walter et al., 2020). Linvill and Warren (2020), for example, found different trolls masquerading in different accounts, including fabricated personas of conservatives, liberals, nonpolitical users participating mostly in popular online hashtag games, and suppliers of news information. Walter et al. (2020) and Freelon et al. (2020) found specific thematic personas pretending to be African American users, talking about a range of sub-group specific topics, from politics (e.g., Black Lives Matter) to culture (e.g., African American musicians).

The prevalence of nonpolitical activity by IRA accounts was explained by Golovchenko et al. (2020) as a form of prepropaganda, a common practice in pre-digital propaganda (Ellul & Kellen, 1973), which is designed to lay the group for future persuasion by making audiences receptive to the source of propaganda. Ellul and Kellen (1973) explicated the meaning of such efforts using the metaphor of plowing the earth before planting flowers. For example, American radio transmissions in Communist countries played popular music to capture the attention of their audiences and gain credibility before promoting pro-West political messages. As Golovchenko et al. (2020) argue, the same tactic was utilized by IRA operatives who gained online popularity and following by first discussing benign nonpolitical issues and news, before shifting their attention to electoral politics.

Once again, the almost exclusive scholarly focus on Russian (and to a lesser degree, Chinese) activity raises the question of whether other foreign campaigns on Twitter used similarly sophisticated tactics, such as the use of prepropaganda. We asked: RQ2: How did foreign countries' campaigns differ in the volume of their online English language propaganda on Twitter?

Human Communication Research, (2023), Vol. 49,No. 4 RQ3: How did foreign countries' campaigns differ in their efforts to create credible personas using nonpolitical information in their online English language propaganda on Twitter?

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: We obtained the data from online campaigns of all six foreign countries campaigns operating in the United States and released by Twitter Moderation Research Consortium. 3 We downloaded campaign activity identified by Twitter as originating in China, Iran, Russia, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Venezuela. These datasets were provided by Twitter and offer the most comprehensive public archive of data related to foreign online campaigns available as of the writing of this article. As with any secondary data, we acknowledge potential advantages and disadvantages for using data collected and curated by a third party. Twitter is an American commercial entity, and the company was engaged in regulatory discussions concerning foreign propaganda in recent years. Some bias may have been present in the curation of the corpora, especially in terms of which foreign actors were tracked (i.e., mostly states that are in adversarial or tensed relationship with the United States). Moreover, Twitter does not provide a full description of the ways data were identified, collected, and curated, thus making it harder for scholars to validate the corpora.

Despite these limitations, the Twitter data have considerable advantages for research into online propaganda. Most importantly, the Twitter Moderation Research Consortium has access to server-side information and metadata that are not publicly available. While regular end-users (including academic researchers who approach Twitter data via the Academic API) may be able to ascertain reported location, or even the platform used to engage with Twitter (e.g., Android or Iphone), the company itself has valuable additional information, from IP addresses to actual software used that shed additional light on the legitimacy or suspicious nature of activity and accounts. It is also important to remember that when hostile activity is identified, Twitter often removes the content from the website and public archives, making it inaccessible to researchers without a use of their publicly released datasets. Analyses of propaganda that wish to rely on the API are thus limited to tweets and accounts that evaded detection, which could explain why most available off-the-shelf tools for identifying malicious activities target only bots and not the harder-to-detect trolls.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: After downloading the data from Twitter, we performed text processing, removing non-English tweets identified using Google's Compact Language Detector 2, an advanced analytical tool for determining the language in which a text was written, that is used by popular applications such as the Google Chrome browser and has been validated in prior work (Gudivada, 2018). To keep only tweets with substantial thematic information, we also removed 24,603 extremely short tweets with less than 10 characters (after removal of links and picture links). We ended up with 9,658,558 tweets, originating from China (2,094,062), Iran (1,833,204), Russia (3,543,107),Saudi Arabia (376,718), UAE (211,516),and Venezuela (1,593,951).

Next, we analyzed the topics in the combined corpus using topic modeling, a semi-automated, unsupervised method for the analysis of textual data (Blei et al., 2003;Maier et al., 2018). Topic models use a Bayesian generative approach, in which the model attempts to mimic the writing process of a given corpus of documents, to uncover underlying topic and word distributions. In other words, the algorithm extracting a set of topics from which the given corpus could have been created. In this regard, topics are a set of frequency distributions of words, based on the linguistic assumption of cooccurrence, that is, that words that are being used more frequently in the same documents also associate thematically. In topic models, every word in the corpus has a probability of appearing in each of the topics, and every document is composed as a mixture of all topics. A review of topic modeling, as well as best practices for research, can be found in Maier et al. (2018) review.

To improve model estimation, we first removed all duplicate tweets from our corpus, resulting in a modeled corpus of 5,490,458. Duplicates were later re-introduced into the corpus in order to measure the prevalence of various topics and themes. Additional preprocessing was carried in accordance with suggested best practices (Maier et al., 2018) including the removal of stopwords, punctuations and numbers, lowercasing, and removal of all extremely common words (more than 50% of documents) or extremely rare words (less than 0.0001% of the documents). We refrained from stemming (reducing inflected words to their word stem, base or root form) due to its possible deleterious effect on model quality (Schofield & Mimno, 2016).

Especially with shorter text, hyperparameter (indicating the inclination of texts to use either one or multiple topics) and parameter (k, number of topics) optimization is extremely important. We used a fivefold cross-validation over a random 10% sample of our corpus. We ran models with varying number of topics (k) from 2 to 100, and with varying levels of the hyperparameter a, varying between 50/k and 1/k. Using the perplexity measure of model quality (Walter et al., 2020), we chose our final model based on a calculation of the maximum point for the second derivative of all perplexity score changes, at which enlarging the number of topics starts offering diminishing returns to model quality. For sensitivity analysis, we used four indicators for k parameter tuning (Jacobi et al., 2016), which yielded similar results as the perplexity test. Lastly, the authors compared the face validity of the chosen model with three alternative candidates, and found it to be the most adequate for analysis. The final model was set to have k ¼ 66 and a ¼ 0.05.

The final topic model was analyzed by two independent coders, who are experts in online propaganda, who interpreted and labeled the topics. To interpret and label topics, coders examined the words with the highest loading on each topic, the words that are both prevalent and exclusive to each topic (FREX words), and the full tweets that were most representative of each topic (at least 30 tweets per topic, and often more). Labels were then compared, disagreements discussed, and final names were agreed upon.

Our next step was to group topics together into larger coherent themes based on the Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMN) approach (Walter & Ophir, 2019). Developed for the analysis of big data corpora, ANTMN combines four analytical steps: topic modeling, network analysis, community detection, and in-depth qualitative reading. ANTMN is an unsupervised machine learning approach, which fits best with inductive, data-driven research, and was successfully employed in studies into online content, and particularly social media (Bayer et al., 2022;Chen, 2022;Ghosh et al., 2022;Hwang et al., 2022;Lokmanoglu et al., 2022;Ophir et al., 2022;Walter et al., 2022). As explained above, the first stage relied on LDA topic modeling, which at the second stage was modeled into a network of topics. This was done by calculating relationships between topics, and specifically pairwise cosine similarities between topics based on the documents they share. Next, based on the theta-cosine matrix, we constructed a network in which topics served as nodes and similarities between them as edges. The result is a fully connected, undirected, weighted network, where edges provide information on the extent to which topics are related (between 0 and 1). To eliminate such nonsignificant edges, and to reduce the density of the network, we used the backbone method suggested by Serrano et al. (2009) for extracting the multiscale backbone of complex weighted networks. We reduced the network to the least dense yet fully connected version.

Next, we employed the Louvain community detection algorithm (Blondel et al., 2008) to identify the topic communities in our topic network. The same coders then reviewed these groups of topics, to which we refer as "themes", to label the shared meaning of all topics in the group. Lastly, to address RQ1 and RQ3 we averaged the theme prominence for each country over each date. The topic model and theme analysis are presented in Figure 1 in the Results section.

After downloading the data from Twitter, we performed text processing, removing non-English tweets identified using Google's Compact Language Detector 2, an advanced analytical tool for determining the language in which a text was written, that is used by popular applications such as the Google Chrome browser and has been validated in prior work (Gudivada, 2018). To keep only tweets with substantial thematic information, we also removed 24,603 extremely short tweets with less than 10 characters (after removal of links and picture links). We ended up with 9,658,558 tweets, originating from China (2,094,062), Iran (1,833,204), Russia (3,543,107),Saudi Arabia (376,718), UAE (211,516),and Venezuela (1,593,951).

Next, we analyzed the topics in the combined corpus using topic modeling, a semi-automated, unsupervised method for the analysis of textual data (Blei et al., 2003;Maier et al., 2018). Topic models use a Bayesian generative approach, in which the model attempts to mimic the writing process of a given corpus of documents, to uncover underlying topic and word distributions. In other words, the algorithm extracting a set of topics from which the given corpus could have been created. In this regard, topics are a set of frequency distributions of words, based on the linguistic assumption of cooccurrence, that is, that words that are being used more frequently in the same documents also associate thematically. In topic models, every word in the corpus has a probability of appearing in each of the topics, and every document is composed as a mixture of all topics. A review of topic modeling, as well as best practices for research, can be found in Maier et al. (2018) review.

To improve model estimation, we first removed all duplicate tweets from our corpus, resulting in a modeled corpus of 5,490,458. Duplicates were later re-introduced into the corpus in order to measure the prevalence of various topics and themes. Additional preprocessing was carried in accordance with suggested best practices (Maier et al., 2018) including the removal of stopwords, punctuations and numbers, lowercasing, and removal of all extremely common words (more than 50% of documents) or extremely rare words (less than 0.0001% of the documents). We refrained from stemming (reducing inflected words to their word stem, base or root form) due to its possible deleterious effect on model quality (Schofield & Mimno, 2016).

Especially with shorter text, hyperparameter (indicating the inclination of texts to use either one or multiple topics) and parameter (k, number of topics) optimization is extremely important. We used a fivefold cross-validation over a random 10% sample of our corpus. We ran models with varying number of topics (k) from 2 to 100, and with varying levels of the hyperparameter a, varying between 50/k and 1/k. Using the perplexity measure of model quality (Walter et al., 2020), we chose our final model based on a calculation of the maximum point for the second derivative of all perplexity score changes, at which enlarging the number of topics starts offering diminishing returns to model quality. For sensitivity analysis, we used four indicators for k parameter tuning (Jacobi et al., 2016), which yielded similar results as the perplexity test. Lastly, the authors compared the face validity of the chosen model with three alternative candidates, and found it to be the most adequate for analysis. The final model was set to have k ¼ 66 and a ¼ 0.05.

The final topic model was analyzed by two independent coders, who are experts in online propaganda, who interpreted and labeled the topics. To interpret and label topics, coders examined the words with the highest loading on each topic, the words that are both prevalent and exclusive to each topic (FREX words), and the full tweets that were most representative of each topic (at least 30 tweets per topic, and often more). Labels were then compared, disagreements discussed, and final names were agreed upon.

The result is a fully connected, undirected, weighted network, where edges provide information on the extent to which topics are related (between 0 and 1). To eliminate such nonsignificant edges, and to reduce the density of the network, we used the backbone method suggested by Serrano et al. (2009) for extracting the multiscale backbone of complex weighted networks. We reduced the network to the least dense yet fully connected version.

Next, we employed the Louvain community detection algorithm (Blondel et al., 2008) to identify the topic communities in our topic network. The same coders then reviewed these groups of topics, to which we refer as "themes", to label the shared meaning of all topics in the group. sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: RQ1 focused on the topics and issues with which the six campaigns engaged. We compared the difference in salience of the various political themes found in the ANTMN analysis using ANOVA over all six countries. RQ2 explored differences in volume of activity. For this purpose, we counted the number of tweets posted by users belonging to each of these campaigns and compared them over countries.

RQ3 examined pre-propaganda efforts (Ellul & Kellen, 1973;Golovchenko et al., 2020). To address this question, we examined both the deployment of 'infrastructure' (i.e., the manufacture of Twitter accounts) and the content personas discussed. To examine the deployment of networks of fabricated accounts, for each campaign we observed the time in which each account was first created. We estimate the breadth of campaign activity as the cumulative number of active accounts operating for the campaign at each moment, and examining its growth rate. This allowed us to observe whether campaigns took a more a cautious route, slowly creating a network of interconnected users, or a more rushed or precarious one.

We then examined the thematic content used by each persona type (Walter et al., 2020) to examine the salience of political and pre-propaganda nonpolitical themes. In practical terms, we relied here on the product of the ANTMN model, focusing not only on political content but on the full range of discourses. Thus, in addition to studying when accounts were created and at what rate, we also examine their content, with a particular attention to pre-propaganda (nonpolitical) discourse. The use or lack thereof of nonpolitical content can indicate the level of caution and meticulous organization taken by each campaign (Ellul & Kellen, 1973).

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: RQ1 asked what topics and issues various countries' campaigns focused on when engaging with English-speaking audiences. Our topic model identified 66 topics in our corpus. Four topics were found to be "boilerplate" topics, that lacked a coherent thematic meaning and consisted of linguistic artifacts or words that are common to all topics and could therefore not be identified as belonging to specific topics (Maier et al., 2018) and four nonsubstantive topics (e.g., requests to follow on Twitter). These eight topics were removed, resulting in 58 topics for the final analysis. Using the ANTMN method (Walter & Ophir, 2019) we clustered these topics into four themes. Figure 1 shows the network of topics:
Our in-depth qualitative analysis of these clusters yielded the following labels:

1) Pink-"Leisure". These topics related to various nonpolitical issues, online games, past time, and Twitter day-to-day activities. These included, for example, discussions of exercise, music and entertainment, technology, art, family, and relationships. This theme mimicked the core online behavior of genuine Twitter user's communications, which fits the content of pre-propaganda. We believe one topic was misattributed to this theme, one about the Ukraine nuclear power crisis. We think this classification error resulted from the discussion of Ukraine taking place at the early stage of the Russian IRA activity, when the trolls exhibited different linguistic behaviors from their more political future counterparts. Scholars argued that the discussion of Ukraine at this early stage was a trial balloon by the Russians who tested the feasibility of their trolling activity (Walter et al., 2020). As a result, the language associated with this topic may have been more similar to other prepropaganda activities, which could lead this topic to be associated with the leisure topics. Our close reading of tweets associated with this topic supports this speculation, as the trolls made a very heavy use of curse words and emotional terms in this particular topic (but not in other political topics later). 2) Gray-"Local US news". Topics in this theme were mostly hard news reports of US crime, sexual assaults, and shootings. Other local news issues were part of this discussion, including weather reports. This theme was largely descriptive and nonopinionated, with users often just sharing links to media reports on crime. 3) Blue-"Politics". This topic related to US political debates and conflicts. It included discussions of Donald Trump, with most trolls disguising as his supporters, by either attacking liberals, using the #MAGA hashtags associated with him, or criticizing the Mueller investigation into his alleged collusion with Russia. Other discussion in this theme focused on the Black Lives Matter movement, social protests, and the US elections. 4) Green-"International". The international theme focused on hard news, and particularly politics and conflicts, outside of the United States, with dedicated topics focusing on China, Gulf and Middle Eastern countries, and Pakistan. These included discussions of specific political affairs, such as the relationships between Israel and Palestine, Pakistani elections and particularly President and candidate Imran Khan, tensions between the West and North Korea or Russia, and more.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: The main dependent variable used in the following analyses was the ANTMN model's estimation of the relative prevalence of specific themes in each document, aggregated on the campaign level. In topic modeling, each document is operationalized as a mixture of all possible topics. Thus, the Y axis in Figure 2 represents the percentage of language used by each campaign that the model estimated as associated with each specific theme. Since this variable is continuous, to address RQ1's inquiry of differences and similarities, we ran a series of ANOVA models to examine whether accounts originating in different countries had focused on different themes.
The results of this analysis can be seen in Figure 2.

Countries differed in their emphasis of the Local and Crime theme [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 77,881, g 2 ¼ 0.04, p < .001]; International politics [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 463,497, g 2 ¼ 0.19, p < .001]; US Politics [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 683,851, g 2 ¼ 0.26, p < .001]; and Leisure [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 200,704, g 2 ¼ 0.09, p < .001].

Several observations can be drawn from the of differences in theme salience among the six countries. First, the theme of US local news and crime was used primarily by Russia, and to a lesser degree Iran, UAE, and Venezuela. China and Saudi Arabia rarely discussed these topics. The International theme was discussed primarily by Iran (more than 35% of discussion) and the UAE (almost 30%). All other countries discussed these topics in less than 20% of their discourse. US politics were particularly prominent among the Venezuelan trolls (more than 40%), were discussed in a moderate amount by the Russians, and appeared more rarely in discussions by trolls from other countries. Finally, the "Leisure" theme was particularly prominent in the Chinese activity, that used these topics in more than 40% of their discourse. To a somewhat lesser degree, the theme was prominent in the activities of Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Russia. Iran used the theme, but less than others, with only a bit more than 25% of activity associated with leisure.

To better corroborate and understand these findings, and to shed further light on the differences in focus between the countries, we also analyzed the most prominent hashtags used by each of the six campaigns. Table 1 presents the most Figure 1. The topic network modeled from the corpus of all English tweets by accounts from six countries. Nodes represent topics; edges represent cooccurrence of topics in documents (calculated using cosine similarity); color represents community membership (using the Louvain algorithm). Network is weighted, and undirected. Edges filtered using the "backbone" method.
prominent hashtags used by each country. The previous observations made on the theme emphasis (Figure 2) are largely supported by this approach.

As Table 1 shows, China often discussed nonpolitical topics, using hashtags like #recipe, #chef, or #iphone. Russia focused on US local news (#news, #sports, #politics, #local, #maga), and so did Venezuela, which combined hashtags around leisure and US politics (#maga, #technology). Iran focused on Middle East politics (#palestine, #israel, #yemen, #gaza). Saudi Arabia focused on its own geographic area (#saudiarabia, #iran, #yemen), as did the UAE (#qatar, #uae, #yemen, #iran).
sections.text:
The main dependent variable used in the following analyses was the ANTMN model's estimation of the relative prevalence of specific themes in each document, aggregated on the campaign level. In topic modeling, each document is operationalized as a mixture of all possible topics. Thus, the Y axis in Figure 2 represents the percentage of language used by each campaign that the model estimated as associated with each specific theme. Since this variable is continuous, to address RQ1's inquiry of differences and similarities, we ran a series of ANOVA models to examine whether accounts originating in different countries had focused on different themes.

The results of this analysis can be seen in Figure 2.

Countries differed in their emphasis of the Local and Crime theme [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 77,881, g 2 ¼ 0.04, p < .001]; International politics [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 463,497, g 2 ¼ 0.19, p < .001]; US Politics [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 683,851, g 2 ¼ 0.26, p < .001]; and Leisure [F(5, 9,652,552) ¼ 200,704, g 2 ¼ 0.09, p < .001].

Several observations can be drawn from the of differences in theme salience among the six countries. First, the theme of US local news and crime was used primarily by Russia, and to a lesser degree Iran, UAE, and Venezuela. China and Saudi Arabia rarely discussed these topics. The International theme was discussed primarily by Iran (more than 35% of discussion) and the UAE (almost 30%). All other countries discussed these topics in less than 20% of their discourse. US politics were particularly prominent among the Venezuelan trolls (more than 40%), were discussed in a moderate amount by the Russians, and appeared more rarely in discussions by trolls from other countries. Finally, the "Leisure" theme was particularly prominent in the Chinese activity, that used these topics in more than 40% of their discourse. To a somewhat lesser degree, the theme was prominent in the activities of Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Russia. Iran used the theme, but less than others, with only a bit more than 25% of activity associated with leisure.

To better corroborate and understand these findings, and to shed further light on the differences in focus between the countries, we also analyzed the most prominent hashtags used by each of the six campaigns. Table 1 presents the most Figure 1. The topic network modeled from the corpus of all English tweets by accounts from six countries. Nodes represent topics; edges represent cooccurrence of topics in documents (calculated using cosine similarity); color represents community membership (using the Louvain algorithm). Network is weighted, and undirected. Edges filtered using the "backbone" method.

prominent hashtags used by each country. The previous observations made on the theme emphasis (Figure 2) are largely supported by this approach.

As Table 1 shows, China often discussed nonpolitical topics, using hashtags like #recipe, #chef, or #iphone. Russia focused on US local news (#news, #sports, #politics, #local, #maga), and so did Venezuela, which combined hashtags around leisure and US politics (#maga, #technology). Iran focused on Middle East politics (#palestine, #israel, #yemen, #gaza). Saudi Arabia focused on its own geographic area (#saudiarabia, #iran, #yemen), as did the UAE (#qatar, #uae, #yemen, #iran).

sections.header_norm: media utilization deployment and pre propaganda
paragraphs: To examine media utilization, deployment strategies, and prepropaganda, we compared the volume and activity patterns in each campaign. Figure 3 shows the cumulative volume of activity for all campaigns. To increase the readability of the graphs, some early minimal activity predating 2013 was removed from the figure, though the full data were calculated and modeled in ANTMN and is discussed below. In Figure 3, we can see an early, very modest, presence of Chinese activity, that increased only around 2019, and kept on going through 2020. The first actor to mount a massive campaign was Russia, in mid-2014 and focused on messaging related to Ukraine. This campaign was short lived, however. The Russians changed their focus to building credible personas moving forward, later followed by US-oriented political messages around the 2016 elections. The figure also points to a substantial campaign deployed by Venezuela, starting in the end of 2016, and spiking at the end of January 2017. Iran was active in particular in 2017, and the others deployed short-lived campaigns after 2017.

Figure 4 shows striking differences in volume between the six countries. As expected, Russia exhibited the highest amount of activity. Iran and China also showed a high amount of activity followed by Venezuela. An important part of the campaign was the early deployment of Troll accounts and cultivating them before activating them to address political issues (RQ3). Figure 4 plots the volume of activity and the cumulative number of troll accounts created by date. In other words, the number of trolls that were created by the campaign from the beginning of the network creation and up to that point.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: Trolls without borders Venezuela, for example, both completed the deployment of their account network before their activity spiked. Iran exhibited a similar, if less extensive, pattern. One should keep in mind that the campaigns studied did not cease activity when accomplishing their goals, but rather when they were detected by Twitter and removed from the platform. Our data suggest that Twitter often identified the suspicious activity following irregularities in the form of sharp increases in users and activity, which explains why in most networks the peak of activity precedes the termination of data.

Another part of the sophistication of the IRA, which was noted in multiple studies (Broniatowski et al., 2018;Freelon et al., 2020;Golovchenko et al., 2020;Linvill & Warren, 2020;Walter et al., 2020), was bolstering of credibility during a pre-propaganda stage. IRA personas were often constructed by initially focusing not on political topics, but on other mundane issues that regular Twitter users tend to write about, such as work, exercise regiment, family life, and food. We therefore set to examine whether other foreign campaigns took similar approach (RQ3).

To examine the thematic narrative of each case study, we plotted the theme progression for all countries' campaigns over time. Most campaigns took the approach of first crafting credible personas by using the "Leisure" or "News" themes. Only then did these campaigns move to focus more heavily on political topics. This pattern is particularly apparent for Russia and Venezuela. As can be seen in Figure 5, however, one campaign served as the exception to this rule, as Iranian accounts mainly discussed issues of international conflict, without first establishing accounts that offer communication mostly about nonpolitical subjects.

sections.paper_id: Walter and Ophir - 2023 - Trolls without borders a comparative analysis of six foreign countries’ online propaganda campaigns
paragraphs: Similar to the use of radio and films during the golden age of propaganda at the 20th century, contemporary propaganda campaigns rely heavily on the affordances and opportunities brought about by technologies such as the Internet and social media. Studies on the breadth and complexity of social media campaigns focused primarily on activity by the Russia IRA, targeting American audiences before the 2016 elections. Despite its theoretical and practical importance, this line of work was limited in scope and largely ignored the global and varied nature of the phenomenon. Surprisingly, even the massive campaigns organized by China, Iran, and Venezuela received little scholarly attention to date. This study begins to fill that scientific gap by offering a systematic comparative analysis of the contemporary Twitter propaganda activity by six countries, all identified by Twitter as malicious propaganda, and released for researchers to study.

Our analysis of potential propaganda topics identified four distinct themes used across the six campaigns, which we labeled leisure, international, US politics, and local and crime News. Campaigns differed in thematic focus. While some, such as the ones originating in Russia and Venezuela, targeted domestic US affairs, China, focused on leisure and US local news, and Saudi Arabia and Iran focused on international conflicts. As for utilization strategies, most countries deployed their accounts ahead of their political activity, presumably harnessing pre-propaganda to bolster the credibility of their users and gather Twitter followers. Iran was the only country to largely ignore pre-propaganda at the early stage of its campaign, which instead immediately focused on political messaging, supported by only a modest discussion of leisure. Both the analysis of content and diffusion strategies demonstrate that the Russian propaganda model, with its focus on the Figure 5. Changing emphasis in themes for the six countries over time. Note that the timeline for different operations is not identical, as some initiated their campaign earlier than others, and as some had more prolonged campaigns that were terminated by Twitter later.
United States and pre-propaganda, was used by some, but not all, countries.

The finding that propaganda campaigns aimed at multiple issues could guide future research into the effects of online propaganda. Prior studies failed to find empirical evidence for effects of IRA propaganda on the American public opinion and political behavior (Bail et al., 2020). However, it might be the case that effects were absent due to the scholarly focus on attempts to persuade Americans to change their beliefs and attitudes on US politics. Specifically, it might be the case that Americans who were targeted by Russian trolls communicating primarily about US politics held firm prior opinions on US politics and politicians prior to exposure to the Russian messages, and were therefore less susceptible to opinion change. Communications about other political contexts that are less familiar to Americans, like the tensions between Pakistan and India around Kashmir, which was discussed in our corpus, may yield stronger effects on American audiences (Morley & Walker, 1987). However, it should be noted that we are unable to fully ascertain the target populations for these campaigns aside from them being part of the English-speaking World. Future empirical research would be able to shed more light on that hypothesis.

From a theoretical perspective, our findings highlight the lack of self-evident connection between two distinct concepts, the target country, the citizens of which the campaign hope to persuade, and the focus of the propaganda content, that is mentioned in the actual campaign messages. While in IRA activity the two seem to have been the same-Americans and US politics, our data demonstrate cases where campaigns attempted to influence public opinion of English-speaking populations around issues related to a third country, for example Iran's attempt to promote arguments about Israel among English-speaking audiences. This distinction is critical, as we propose that target audiences will be more susceptible to persuasion when it comes to messages about other countries, as existing research shows that media effects can be larger when the audience have little direct experience with the object targeted by the media content (Bai & McCombs, 2007).

We believe that exploring multiple countries in a comparative fashion substantially improves our understanding of digital propaganda. We expected differences between campaigns based on a number of factors. First, countries may vary in their political goals and motivations, based on geo-political histories and context. Crucially, some of the countries studied here maintain international cooperation with the United States and the West, while others are considered adversaries at the time of writing. Second, some countries are involved in more than one digital propaganda campaign, with the United States not being their sole or even main target. Third, campaigns differ in the amount and quality of available resources dedicated to propaganda in English. Taken together, we believe that our comparative analysis of campaigns, and particularly ones that received very little scholarly attention before, could improve our theoretical understanding of digital propaganda, while also helping intelligence agencies prepare for future online interferences.

This study has several limitations. First, we only examined English communications. The data released by Twitter contained non-English messages as well, that are likely to differ in content and focus, and aim at local, rather than foreign, audiences. In the case of the IRA, for example, evidence shows that earlier activity was geared towards domestic Russian audiences rather than Western ones (Woolley, 2016). The move from domestic to international propaganda should be a fertile ground for future studies.

Second, content analysis alone could not reveal effects on audiences. Future studies would be able to build upon our findings and examine the actual effects on audiences, and examine, for example, our hypothesis that effects may be more pronounced when audiences' familiarity with political issues discussed is low. Third, our corpus was limited to one social media platform, Twitter, which does not represent the whole population of online users or of propaganda efforts. Nevertheless, at least at the time of data collection, Twitter served as one of the only major platforms to cooperate with researchers and release datasets identified as foreign propaganda (though it remains unclear whether such cooperation will continue in the future). Relatedly, our data represent discourse at a specific point in time. The social meaning of issues and debates may change over time and researchers employing similar methods in the future should be sensitive to such dynamics. For example, a topic like vaccines, that did not split the American electorate across political lines during the studied time period (Walter et al., 2020) had since become highly politicized in terms of both attitudes and behaviors, with a growing resistance among the American Right (Walter et al., 2022).

Finally, the data were provided by the Twitter Moderation Research Consortium. As with any secondary data, we could not directly control the identification and curation processes. For example, it is plausible that additional campaigns and accounts were missed or not collected by Twitter. We also cannot validate the classification of malicious and genuine users, as Twitter does not reveal the exact method used to identify and tag accounts as problematic. This means that while differences found in content and deployment might be the result of differences between the campaigns, they might be biased by the specific case studies, or the identification processes used by Twitter's moderation team. Despite these important caveats, we follow other scholars by arguing that the data released by Twitter for research purposes is scholarly valuable and unique. As discussed earlier, while imperfect, the Twitter data have considerable advantages for online propaganda research, most notably the company's access to serverside information that is not publicly available, which is crucial for separating legitimate users from trolls. Moreover, as Twitter regularly removes malicious content and block propaganda accounts, the data could not be accessed via the Twitter API.

In sum, we argue that the extension of traditional propaganda theory to the examination of contemporary online propaganda could benefit from a comparative, nuanced approach that acknowledges the phenomenon's complexity, as evident in our corpus in terms of variance in propaganda focus and deployment strategies. Our results show that online campaigns vary in the issues they choose to engage with, as well as their methods of operation (e.g., in terms of prepropaganda). We argue that these results suggest that prior focus on a single campaign could have hindered our understanding of the deployment, content, and effects of online campaigns on audiences, as well as discussions of adequate policies to address the dangers of such campaigns.

In sum, we argue that the extension of traditional propaganda theory to the examination of contemporary online propaganda could benefit from a comparative, nuanced approach that acknowledges the phenomenon's complexity, as evident in our corpus in terms of variance in propaganda focus and deployment strategies. Our results show that online campaigns vary in the issues they choose to engage with, as well as their methods of operation (e.g., in terms of prepropaganda). We argue that these results suggest that prior focus on a single campaign could have hindered our understanding of the deployment, content, and effects of online campaigns on audiences, as well as discussions of adequate policies to address the dangers of such campaigns.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
Similar to the use of radio and films during the golden age of propaganda at the 20th century, contemporary propaganda campaigns rely heavily on the affordances and opportunities brought about by technologies such as the Internet and social media. Studies on the breadth and complexity of social media campaigns focused primarily on activity by the Russia IRA, targeting American audiences before the 2016 elections. Despite its theoretical and practical importance, this line of work was limited in scope and largely ignored the global and varied nature of the phenomenon. Surprisingly, even the massive campaigns organized by China, Iran, and Venezuela received little scholarly attention to date. This study begins to fill that scientific gap by offering a systematic comparative analysis of the contemporary Twitter propaganda activity by six countries, all identified by Twitter as malicious propaganda, and released for researchers to study.

Our analysis of potential propaganda topics identified four distinct themes used across the six campaigns, which we labeled leisure, international, US politics, and local and crime News. Campaigns differed in thematic focus. While some, such as the ones originating in Russia and Venezuela, targeted domestic US affairs, China, focused on leisure and US local news, and Saudi Arabia and Iran focused on international conflicts. As for utilization strategies, most countries deployed their accounts ahead of their political activity, presumably harnessing pre-propaganda to bolster the credibility of their users and gather Twitter followers. Iran was the only country to largely ignore pre-propaganda at the early stage of its campaign, which instead immediately focused on political messaging, supported by only a modest discussion of leisure. Both the analysis of content and diffusion strategies demonstrate that the Russian propaganda model, with its focus on the Figure 5. Changing emphasis in themes for the six countries over time. Note that the timeline for different operations is not identical, as some initiated their campaign earlier than others, and as some had more prolonged campaigns that were terminated by Twitter later.

United States and pre-propaganda, was used by some, but not all, countries.

The finding that propaganda campaigns aimed at multiple issues could guide future research into the effects of online propaganda. Prior studies failed to find empirical evidence for effects of IRA propaganda on the American public opinion and political behavior (Bail et al., 2020). However, it might be the case that effects were absent due to the scholarly focus on attempts to persuade Americans to change their beliefs and attitudes on US politics. Specifically, it might be the case that Americans who were targeted by Russian trolls communicating primarily about US politics held firm prior opinions on US politics and politicians prior to exposure to the Russian messages, and were therefore less susceptible to opinion change. Communications about other political contexts that are less familiar to Americans, like the tensions between Pakistan and India around Kashmir, which was discussed in our corpus, may yield stronger effects on American audiences (Morley & Walker, 1987). However, it should be noted that we are unable to fully ascertain the target populations for these campaigns aside from them being part of the English-speaking World. Future empirical research would be able to shed more light on that hypothesis.

From a theoretical perspective, our findings highlight the lack of self-evident connection between two distinct concepts, the target country, the citizens of which the campaign hope to persuade, and the focus of the propaganda content, that is mentioned in the actual campaign messages. While in IRA activity the two seem to have been the same-Americans and US politics, our data demonstrate cases where campaigns attempted to influence public opinion of English-speaking populations around issues related to a third country, for example Iran's attempt to promote arguments about Israel among English-speaking audiences. This distinction is critical, as we propose that target audiences will be more susceptible to persuasion when it comes to messages about other countries, as existing research shows that media effects can be larger when the audience have little direct experience with the object targeted by the media content (Bai & McCombs, 2007).

We believe that exploring multiple countries in a comparative fashion substantially improves our understanding of digital propaganda. We expected differences between campaigns based on a number of factors. First, countries may vary in their political goals and motivations, based on geo-political histories and context. Crucially, some of the countries studied here maintain international cooperation with the United States and the West, while others are considered adversaries at the time of writing. Second, some countries are involved in more than one digital propaganda campaign, with the United States not being their sole or even main target. Third, campaigns differ in the amount and quality of available resources dedicated to propaganda in English. Taken together, we believe that our comparative analysis of campaigns, and particularly ones that received very little scholarly attention before, could improve our theoretical understanding of digital propaganda, while also helping intelligence agencies prepare for future online interferences.

This study has several limitations. First, we only examined English communications. The data released by Twitter contained non-English messages as well, that are likely to differ in content and focus, and aim at local, rather than foreign, audiences. In the case of the IRA, for example, evidence shows that earlier activity was geared towards domestic Russian audiences rather than Western ones (Woolley, 2016). The move from domestic to international propaganda should be a fertile ground for future studies.

Second, content analysis alone could not reveal effects on audiences. Future studies would be able to build upon our findings and examine the actual effects on audiences, and examine, for example, our hypothesis that effects may be more pronounced when audiences' familiarity with political issues discussed is low. Third, our corpus was limited to one social media platform, Twitter, which does not represent the whole population of online users or of propaganda efforts. Nevertheless, at least at the time of data collection, Twitter served as one of the only major platforms to cooperate with researchers and release datasets identified as foreign propaganda (though it remains unclear whether such cooperation will continue in the future). Relatedly, our data represent discourse at a specific point in time. The social meaning of issues and debates may change over time and researchers employing similar methods in the future should be sensitive to such dynamics. For example, a topic like vaccines, that did not split the American electorate across political lines during the studied time period (Walter et al., 2020) had since become highly politicized in terms of both attitudes and behaviors, with a growing resistance among the American Right (Walter et al., 2022).

Finally, the data were provided by the Twitter Moderation Research Consortium. As with any secondary data, we could not directly control the identification and curation processes. For example, it is plausible that additional campaigns and accounts were missed or not collected by Twitter. We also cannot validate the classification of malicious and genuine users, as Twitter does not reveal the exact method used to identify and tag accounts as problematic. This means that while differences found in content and deployment might be the result of differences between the campaigns, they might be biased by the specific case studies, or the identification processes used by Twitter's moderation team. Despite these important caveats, we follow other scholars by arguing that the data released by Twitter for research purposes is scholarly valuable and unique. As discussed earlier, while imperfect, the Twitter data have considerable advantages for online propaganda research, most notably the company's access to serverside information that is not publicly available, which is crucial for separating legitimate users from trolls. Moreover, as Twitter regularly removes malicious content and block propaganda accounts, the data could not be accessed via the Twitter API.

In sum, we argue that the extension of traditional propaganda theory to the examination of contemporary online propaganda could benefit from a comparative, nuanced approach that acknowledges the phenomenon's complexity, as evident in our corpus in terms of variance in propaganda focus and deployment strategies. Our results show that online campaigns vary in the issues they choose to engage with, as well as their methods of operation (e.g., in terms of prepropaganda). We argue that these results suggest that prior focus on a single campaign could have hindered our understanding of the deployment, content, and effects of online campaigns on audiences, as well as discussions of adequate policies to address the dangers of such campaigns.
